description: A traveller accepts a neighbour's offer of shelter and steps inside. 'kelomi' willingly takes up what is offered or makes room for what is presented. A person may accept a gift or method, take up a condition, or welcome another participant. None of those choices agrees with every claim or grants blanket consent to later acts.
articulatory_notes: Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release into middle-height front unrounded 'e'. Touch the tongue tip to the alveolar ridge for voiced 'l', let air pass along its sides, and round the lips for stressed middle-height back 'o'. Close the lips for voiced 'm', keep the airflow nasal, and finish with high-front unrounded 'i'.
usage_notes: Whatever is accepted is the direct object, and a complete proposition may use the 'mena ... meno' frame. The event-noun rule supplies acceptance. 'howela' (receive) reports arrival into someone's possession whether welcome or not, 'nawo' (agree) reports a shared position, and 'lesawi' (consent) gives bounded permission for an action affecting oneself or a relationship for which one may speak.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Practice may begin by accepting a present condition clearly enough to work with it. Acceptance here neither praises the condition nor asks a person to leave preventable harm unchanged.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Accepting another person's presence or participation can make room for relationship without pretending that every belief, request, or action has also been accepted.

semantic_domains.activity: taking up an offered object, role, condition, or course
semantic_domains.community: admitting another person's presence or participation
